title: FOOSH on the ice
author: Handcraft seed
stem: A 58-year-old woman slipped on icy steps this morning and fell onto her outstretched left hand. She presents to the ED with wrist pain and an obvious deformity.

chief_complaint.label: Chief complaint
chief_complaint.content: 58F, left wrist pain and deformity after FOOSH.

history.label: History
history.content: Slip on ice ~2 hours ago, fell onto outstretched left hand. Immediate pain and deformity. No LOC, no other injuries. Right-hand dominant. Retired teacher, gardener. PMH: hypertension, osteopenia (last DEXA 2 years ago). Meds: amlodipine, calcium/D. No anticoagulation. NKDA. Tetanus current.

exam.label: Physical exam
exam.content: Left wrist: classic 'dinner fork' deformity with dorsal angulation. Moderate swelling. Skin intact (closed). Tender along distal radius. Limited active ROM, pain-limited. Median nerve: sensation slightly diminished over volar thumb, index, long fingers compared to contralateral side; reports pins-and-needles. Radial pulse 2+. Capillary refill <2s. Compartments soft.

imaging.label: Imaging
imaging.content: PA and lateral wrist radiographs: extra-articular distal radius fracture with 25° dorsal angulation, 6mm radial shortening, and 18° radial inclination loss. No intra-articular extension. No DRUJ widening. Concurrent ulnar styloid fracture (non-displaced).

management.label: Management
management.content: Diagnosis: Closed distal radius fracture (extra-articular, dorsally displaced) with acute median nerve symptoms — concerning for acute carpal tunnel syndrome. Initial plan: Hematoma block + closed reduction in ED, sugar-tong splint. Re-examine median nerve post-reduction — if symptoms resolve, observe; if persistent, urgent carpal tunnel release. Repeat radiographs to confirm acceptable alignment. Hand/ortho follow-up within 1 week. Definitive management likely operative (volar locked plate) given displacement, age, functional demand, and incomplete restoration with reduction is common in this pattern. Discuss osteoporosis workup.

pearls.text: Median nerve symptoms after a distal radius fracture demand re-examination after reduction. Persistent symptoms = urgent carpal tunnel release, not a watch-and-wait.
A distal radius fracture in a 58-year-old woman is a sentinel event. Recommend DEXA and consider osteoporosis workup — the next fracture is often the hip.

teachingPoints: Acceptable closed reduction parameters (general): <5° dorsal angulation, <5mm radial shortening, articular step-off <2mm. These tighten with younger, higher-demand patients.
DRUJ instability changes the surgical plan — always assess.
Median nerve symptoms before reduction may resolve with reduction; symptoms that persist or develop after reduction are an emergency.
Volar locked plating is the most common contemporary fixation for displaced extra-articular fractures requiring surgery.
references: AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline on Distal Radius Fractures
Wolfe et al., Green's Operative Hand Surgery — distal radius chapter

reasoningAutopsy.shouldHaveNoticedEarly: Two things require your attention before reduction: the median nerve exam (document baseline) and the DRUJ (assess stability). Both are commonly missed in the urgency to reduce and splint. Document both before and after reduction.
reasoningAutopsy.whatChangesManagement: Persistent or worsening median nerve symptoms after reduction = urgent carpal tunnel release. DRUJ instability discovered intraoperatively changes fixation strategy. Intra-articular extension (not present here) changes surgical approach. Patient age and functional demand both tighten the acceptable reduction thresholds.
reasoningAutopsy.attendingMayAskNext: What are the acceptable post-reduction parameters on PA and lateral? How do you assess DRUJ stability? When does an older patient's distal radius fracture warrant operative fixation?
reasoningAutopsy.bestRoundsOneLiner: FOOSH, dinner-fork deformity: reduce, check median nerve after, splint and follow in 1 week — persistent nerve symptoms go to the OR tonight.
reasoningAutopsy.commonMistake: Watching persistent median nerve symptoms after reduction instead of acting — and failing to assess the DRUJ, which is a companion injury in dorsally displaced distal radius fractures.
reasoningAutopsy.onePearlToRemember: A distal radius fracture in a 58-year-old woman is a sentinel event for osteoporosis. Recommend DEXA — the next fracture is often the hip.
